goal: Draft v0.3 spec patch: coverage model + seal snapshot contract — unify two MVP-feedback gaps under a single strong invariant (every main commit is covered/skipped/uncovered)

summary.title: v0.3 spec patch: coverage model + seal snapshot contract under one invariant
summary.what: Adds docs_for_ai/mainline-spec-v0.3-coverage-and-snapshot.md (613 lines). Merges two independent dogfood-feedback gaps from the mainline-watch user (seal evidence can be silently incomplete; manual commits create coverage gaps) into a single design under one strong invariant: every commit reachable from main is in exactly one of three states — covered / skipped / uncovered — and the state is a direct function of git facts (notes ref + commit messages + skip-pattern config), with no mainline-private derived schema. Specifies seven patches (A-G) covering prepare/submit invariants, sealed-event schema bump for evidence completeness, the notes-ref-based coverage definition, the dual skip mechanism (Mainline-Skip trailer + [mainline.skip] patterns), status surface, the `mainline gaps` command (deliberately NOT named `reconcile` since that was renamed in v0.2), and `start --commits` as the backfill primitive with `--range` as sugar. Documents rescue priority (reset > backfill > skip > accept) ordered by reversibility. Estimates 4 days to implement.
summary.why: The mainline-watch user surfaced two issues that look independent but are actually two halves of the same gap: a sealed intent does not precisely claim what evidence it covers (worktree dirty / HEAD drift / branch drift can pass silently), AND there is no observable state for "commits that are not claimed by any sealed intent" (manual commits, bot commits, cherry-picks, rebases all leak through). Solving either one alone leaves the other half. Combining them under one invariant — every commit on main is covered/skipped/uncovered — gives a single mental model that explains all the rescue paths and prevents future symptom-chasing API additions like `mainline adopt`. Dogfood feedback before friend MVP rollout makes this the right pre-handoff spec.
summary.user_goal: Have a single coherent design that the next phase-1-feedback (or any future "this thing was missed" feedback) can map onto, instead of growing the API one verb per symptom.

decisions.rationale: The two issues share the same underlying defect class — uncertainty about what is and is not claimed by sealed intents. Splitting them would invite implementations that satisfy each invariant separately but contradict in interaction (e.g., snapshot contract that allows dirty submits + coverage model that treats those as covered). One patch keeps the unified invariant load-bearing.

decisions.point: Coverage source-of-truth: notes ref vs range vs cache
decisions.rationale: Already shipped infrastructure (rc3+). Notes follow commits through rebase / cherry-pick automatically. Multi-intent squash naturally supported via the existing `intents` array on a single note. Range overlap is ambiguous on merge commits and overlapping intent ranges; cache files create derivation drift. Notes ref is the strictly best option.

decisions.point: Skip mechanism: trailer vs config vs both
decisions.rationale: Trailer is per-commit auditable; config is convenient for whole-class skips (chore: format, Merge branch). Empty reason rejected on the trailer side specifically to prevent "Mainline-Skip:" becoming a thoughtless rubber stamp. Conflict resolution between covered and skipped: covered wins (sealed intent is a stronger claim than a skip pattern).

decisions.point: --allow-dirty flag vs config bypass for snapshot contract
decisions.chose: CLI flag at every callsite, NO config option
decisions.rationale: Config-level safety bypasses become CI time bombs ("oh we set it once, forgot why"). CLI flag forces re-affirmation per submit. The dirty fact still propagates into the sealed event so the audit trail records "this seal was made dirty" forever — escape hatch is loud, not silent.

decisions.point: New backfill verb vs reuse start
decisions.rationale: The user explicitly pushed back against adding `mainline adopt`. start --commits keeps the lifecycle (start → append → prepare → submit) identical and only changes the input — which commits the intent will eventually pin to. New verb would be a parallel pipeline that drifts from the main flow over time.

`reconcile` was just renamed to `pin` in v0.2 because of semantic murkiness; reusing it here would erase the rename benefit. `coverage` sounds like a config inspection command. `gaps` is direct and matches the status summary line ("⚠ Uncovered: 1").

rejected.alternative: Implement v0.3 in this PR alongside the spec
rejected.reason: Estimated 4 days work covering 6 implementation steps with cross-package changes. Would slow reviewer feedback on the design itself, which is the load-bearing artifact.

rejected.alternative: Add `mainline adopt` as the backfill command
rejected.reason: Explicit user pushback. New verbs are cheap to add and expensive to remove; keep the API minimal until coverage-model dogfood data justifies a new entry point.
Derivation drift risk. Coverage is a direct function of notes ref + commit messages; recomputing on read with cat-file --batch (already shipped) is cheap (<200ms target).
Configuration-level safety bypasses become silent failure modes; explicit user pushback. Use --allow-dirty CLI flag instead — re-affirm per call.

rejected.alternative: Retroactive "skip note" parallel to commit trailer
rejected.reason: Two sources of truth (commit message vs note) for the same fact would inevitably drift. Limit skip to the commit message; no retroactive mechanism.

risks: Notes-ref-based coverage assumes the team uses the auto-pin / explicit-pin flow correctly. A commit that has manually-deleted notes (`git notes remove`) will appear uncovered even if its underlying intent exists. Acceptable: deleting notes is an explicit destructive op, treating it as ground truth is correct.

Force-push to main rewrites notes-attached commits; old notes dangle unless team also pushes notes ref carefully. Documented as "use git push --follow-tags or explicit notes refspec" in AGENTS.md (already covered in existing fetch refspec config).

The skip-pattern regex is matched against commit subject only. A multi-line commit message with `Mainline-Skip:` mid-body could be missed if implementation only reads subject. Implementation MUST use `git interpret-trailers` style parsing (last paragraph), not subject-only.

Spec is design only; user-visible behavior change is in the implementation PRs that follow. Risk that 4-day estimate slips if integration tests reveal interaction with auto-pin-on-sync (pin runs inside sync; if status is computed before pin completes, the count is stale by one).

The HEAD-equality check in submit assumes the agent is using a CLI driver that reads HEAD freshly per submit. If a long-lived process holds prepare output in memory and the user manually rebases between prepare and submit, submit fails — this is desired (correctness over convenience), but the error message must be clear about the cause.
followups: Implement v0.3 per §10 (6 steps, ~4 days). Each step is independently testable; can land as separate PRs if review bandwidth requires.
After dogfood: profile whether `mainline status` < 200ms target holds with 1000+ commits in coverage window; consider sliding-window cache if not.

Spec section §12 lists future evolutions deferred to post-v0.3: temp ref for prepare (instead of JSON-embedded head), coverage trends over time, auto-suggest skip pattern. Each is a small follow-up PR if dogfood data motivates.
Update AGENTS.md (template at internal/engine/templates/agents-md.md) with the rescue priority table and Mainline-Skip trailer convention once Step 6 lands.

architectural_claims: Every commit on main is in exactly one of three states: covered (notes ref points at sealed intent), skipped (trailer or pattern match), uncovered (neither). The state is computable directly from git facts.
Sealed intent claim is precise and tamper-evident: HEAD/branch/worktree are checked at submit, and worktree status is permanently recorded in the sealed event.
Backfill (start --commits) reuses the normal lifecycle; no parallel adopt pipeline.

api_changes.signature: seal --prepare schema_version 1->2 (new snapshot block: changed_files, worktree_status, evidence_complete, current_branch)
mainline status default shows Coverage section
mainline seal --submit gains --allow-dirty flag and HEAD/branch/worktree invariant checks

migration_notes: Schema bump for seal --prepare and IntentSealedEvent are additive; v0.2 readers still work via legacy-default fallbacks.
Existing repositories: no migration step needed; coverage state is computed from existing notes ref + commit messages.
